content.assessment: The Finnish experience shows that presumption-against-closure protections are politically reversible policy choices, not automatic features of any given governance model. Finland's low pupil minimums in the 1980s protected small schools only as long as political will existed; once municipal decentralization and recession pressures hit in 1995-2003, small rural schools declined dramatically despite the comprehensive model's egalitarian foundations. This suggests a codified presumption-against-closure duty in Hungary faces the same durability risk unless paired with independent enforcement, since Finland's own protections eroded once fiscal and administrative incentives shifted. The Finnish sequencing also shows structural safeguards work best when embedded early in comprehensive-system design (piloted in rural areas first) rather than added procedurally after a highly centralized model is already in place, which is Hungary's context.
opportunities: The finding that municipal decentralization in Finland coincided with school decline (F-live-finnish_reform-03) suggests Hungary's centralized structure could, in principle, offer more durable top-down enforcement of a presumption-against-closure duty than Finland's decentralized model allowed.
strengths: Finland demonstrates that presumption-type protections can meaningfully delay closures when politically sustained (F-live-finnish_reform-02), lending plausibility to a codified procedural safeguard.

Finland's 1972-1977 reform legitimized rural viability as a precondition rather than residual concern, a principle-level parallel supporting T1's goal of preventing avoidable closures (F-live-finnish_reform-01).
threats: If Hungary's 2026 governance reform reintroduces municipal or principal input without centralized enforcement teeth, it risks replicating the Finnish pattern where decentralization loosened protective minimums and enabled closures (F-live-finnish_reform-02, F-live-finnish_reform-03).
weaknesses: Finland's own rural-school protections proved reversible once municipal decentralization and recession pressures intervened, indicating procedural or policy safeguards without durable independent enforcement are vulnerable to erosion (F-live-finnish_reform-02, F-live-finnish_reform-03).
Finland's case does not directly test a 'presumption-against-closure' legal duty analogous to Scotland's, so the lens offers only indirect, structural-analogy evidence, not a direct precedent.
